coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and characterization of high-performance forward-osmosis membrane by introducing manganese oxide incited graphene quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: Forward osmosis (FO) is the futuristic membrane desalination technology as it transcends the disadvantages of other pressure-driven techniques. But, there still remain critical challenges like fabrication of highly permeable membrane with ideal structures maintaining high rejection rates that need to be addressed for implementation as a practical technology. In this work, novel thin-film composite (TFC) membranes were fabricated by means of incorporating manganese oxide (MnO2) incited graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposite into a cellulose acetate (CA) suspension followed by phase inversion (PI) for enhanced FO performance. The surface morphology and chemical structure of fabricated membranes were studied using various characterization techniques like XRD, FT-IR, SEM-EDS, Mapping, AFM, and TGA. The structural parameters, water flux, reverse salt flux and salt rejection was estimated on the basis of data obtained from four varying initial draw solution concentrations. At high nanocomposites stacking, the hydrophilicity of the casting blend increase, and subsequently, the PI exchange rate additionally increases, which brings about noticeable difference in the surface morphology. The membrane with 0.5 wt% nanocomposite exhibited superior FO separation performance with osmotic water flux of 18.89, 34.49, 41.76 and 42.34 in L.m−2.h−1 with variable concentrations of NaCl salt solution (0.25M, 0.5M, 1M, and 2M), respectively. Also, the porosity of the membrane was increased to 47.23% with 96.87% salt rejection. The results indicate that the hydrophilicity of the nanocomposite drives them to the interface among CA and water during PI process leading to solid hydrogen bonding to achieve high water permeability.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Forward osmosis (FO) is the futuristic membrane desalination technology as it transcends the disadvantages of other pressure-driven techniques. But, there still remain critical challenges like fabrication of highly permeable membrane with ideal structures maintaining high rejection rates that need to be addressed for implementation as a practical technology. In this work, novel thin-film composite (TFC) membranes were fabricated by means of incorporating manganese oxide (MnO2) incited graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposite into a cellulose acetate (CA) suspension followed by phase inversion (PI) for enhanced FO performance. The surface morphology and chemical structure of fabricated membranes were studied using various characterization techniques like XRD, FT-IR, SEM-EDS, Mapping, AFM, and TGA. The structural parameters, water flux, reverse salt flux and salt rejection was estimated on the basis of data obtained from four varying initial draw solution concentrations. At high nanocomposites stacking, the hydrophilicity of the casting blend increase, and subsequently, the PI exchange rate additionally increases, which brings about noticeable difference in the surface morphology. The membrane with 0.5 wt% nanocomposite exhibited superior FO separation performance with osmotic water flux of 18.89, 34.49, 41.76 and 42.34 in L.m−2.h−1 with variable concentrations of NaCl salt solution (0.25M, 0.5M, 1M, and 2M), respectively. Also, the porosity of the membrane was increased to 47.23% with 96.87% salt rejection. The results indicate that the hydrophilicity of the nanocomposite drives them to the interface among CA and water during PI process leading to solid hydrogen bonding to achieve high water permeability.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is Earth's most valuable asset for survival and is getting dangerously scant and befouled. The extent of the worldwide population utilizing fundamental potable water service expanded from 80 to 89 percent from 2000 to 2015 (Nations, 2019). Attaining global access to clean and readily accessible drinking water by 2030 is a big challenge for all nations, not only those with low incomes (Water, 2018). To address this challenge, there is a significant requirement for the advancement of proficient and supportable innovations to increase water supply past what is possible from the natural hydrological cycle. Membrane based treatment can have a critical part in water sanitization and desalination. These technologies produce water of predominant quality and are less delicate to feed water fluctuations (Aliyu et al., 2018). Furthermore, membrane-based desalination advancements are naturally more energy proficient than thermal methodologies. Membrane technology for desalination and water purification with separation to a great extent dependent on size includes ultrafiltration (UF), microfiltration (MF), nanofiltration (NF), reverse osmosis (RO) and emerging low energy-based FO process (Ahmad et al., 2020). Contrasted with the other membrane techniques, FO as a new membrane technology has exhibited a ton of points of interest such as ease, simple cleaning, low fouling inclination, etc. (Ang et al., 2020; Awad et al., 2019; Wang and Liu, 2021). In this way, gigantic endeavors have been given by membrane researchers to build up the FO membrane technology as a possible desalination method to contend with RO process (Chung et al., 2012; Skuse et al., 2021).

Notwithstanding the incredible push in FO technology, there is still a big question for enhancing the overall characteristics of the thin-film composite (TFC) FO membranes. Polymeric membranes are by a long shot the widespread, generally attributed to their high processability and minimal effort (Sarkar and Chakraborty, 2021). Most of the commercially accessible permeable membranes that are utilized for MF and UF, just as some dense membranes utilized for FO and RO, are cast by phase inversion (PI). This method includes the precipitation of a disintegrated polymer in a thin-film to develop a permeable membrane structure in a controlled manner. The principle PI method utilized is non-solvent-induced phase separation (NIPS), in which a homogenous solution of polymer mixed in solvent is inundated in a non-solvent phase, commonly a deionized water bath, prompting solvent–non-solvent exchange and PI into a solid membrane matrix with porous structure. Other PI methods incorporate solvent evaporation, in which an unstable solvent is permitted to dissipate from a fluid film that incorporates polymer and nonsolvent in a controlled way, and thermal PI, in which an ultra-high temperature polymer solution is gradually cooled until PI happens. During PI, a few key boundaries decide the subsequent film morphology like, the type and concentration of polymer, solvent and non-solvent, and the fabrication method (Deng and Li, 2021; Garcia-Ivars et al., 2014; Wei et al., 2021).

State-of-the-art upgrades in water filtration membranes have been minor due to inalienable material impediments, including the penetrability selectivity tradeoff, which restricts the feasible water solute selectivity, and fouling propensity. Novel and versatile materials, scale-up and sub-atomic level design approach for membrane manufacture are basic for beating these impediments and for considerably propelling water filtration and desalination advances (Jain and Garg, 2021). One of the possible answers for this matter is to use nanotechnology for enhancing the membrane performance (Pendergast and Hoek, 2011). Likewise, nanomaterial aid in defeating the issues of less penetrability, low physical strength, and fouling mechanism, while improving the porousness as well as bringing down the marvel of ICP. The joining of nanomaterials in the substrate of a membrane will build membrane porosity and hydrophilicity while diminishing fouling that by and large mitigate the impact of internal concentration polarization (ICP) (Goh et al., 2016). Metal oxides and functionalized carbon-based nanomaterials are broadly used to achieve this reason. Out of all the carbon-based nanoparticles, graphene oxide subordinates are considered to be more progressively significant because they upgrade the selectivity, execution, and efficiency of the thin-film membrane by modifying the fabrication process (Ng et al., 2013; Sri Abirami Saraswathi et al., 2019).

Manganese dioxide (MnO2) is a natural dark to brown-shaded inorganic compound that occurs as the mineral pyrolusite. The physical and chemical properties of MnO2 is a theme each water purification expert ought to comprehend and be skilled in studying for groundwater treatment applications in their area. Utilization of MnO2 is perhaps the most usually utilized and least comprehended use for eliminating iron, manganese, hydrogen sulfide, arsenic and radium (Chen and He, 2008). It isn't only an enchantment dust, however, a supportive catalyst employed to enhance quality of water from private wells to enormous regions. The utilizations for MnO2, in the same way as other innovations today in water purification, has a long and celebrated history. In water purification, MnO2 furnishes a catalyst as a filter media. Manganese dioxide are favored over other oxides due to its minimal effort, high action, great strength, huge explicit surface area, climate friendly nature, primary adaptability, and solid oxidizing properties which are basic for an adsorbent for water and wastewater treatment (Husnain et al., 2020; Rudder et al., 2004). On the other hand, joining graphene-based nanomaterials in the membrane has been accounted for upgraded water penetrability from half to 200% and high antifouling property. Shen et al. (2016) fabricated a sort of FO membrane by joining graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets with 1,3-phenylenediamine during ultrasonic treatment, that were covalently clung to the polyamide chains. As a result, the water porousness upgraded to about 59% and an improved antifouling inclination was observed for the GO-consolidated membrane contrasted with the unmodified TFC-FO membrane. Likewise, Xu et al. (2019) investigated GQDs-joined TFC membranes for FO applications with a thick 2D sheet morphology with three graphene layers. Covalent bonds were formed among GQDs and polyethyleneimine, hence, enhancing membrane stability during filtration performance. The osmotic filtration performance of the GQDs membrane was found to be improved with appropriate stacking of GQDs. Moreover, the fouling of the nanocomposite membrane was found to be practically reversible which demonstrates a great possibility for osmotic applications.

In this investigation, targeting accomplishing brilliant FO separation performance, novel TFC FO membranes by means of incorporating MnO2 incited GQDs into a cellulose acetate (CA) suspension followed by PI were prepared. CA is the first choice for industrial and domestic application as it can be easily modified in the composite form and blends to obtain desirable properties (Jain et al., 2021b). As of late, a comparative exploration studies have been accounted for NF and FO treatment with various graphene-based loadings, however, none of the studies so far has been endeavored to fuse graphene with MnO2 for developing FO membranes by PI. In present study, the surface morphology along with all the basic properties of synthesized nanomaterials and nanocomposite membranes with comprehensive characterizations were examined. The impacts of different nanocomposite loading on the membranes dynamic structure and on the osmotic performance of TFC FO membrane were also investigated. The hydrophilicity of the nanocomposite drives them to the interface among cellulose and water during PI process. A solid hydrogen bonding among nanocomposite and cellulose was upheld by expanded consistency of the casting solution. Various surface characterizations demonstrated improved morphology and hydrophilicity which confirms the presence and bonding of nanocomposite.

Cellulose acetate (Extra pure, M.W-30,000, CAS no. 9004-35-7) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Inc. Polyvinyl pyrrolidone k-25 (Povidone, PVP) (C6H9N0) (M. W: −10000, CAS no. 9003-39-8) and N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (N-Methyl pyrrolidone, 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone, NMP) (MW: 99.13, CAS no. 872-50-4) were supplied by CDH Pvt Ltd. Manganese chloride (MnCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), lead acetate ((Pb(C2H3O2)2), potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and deionized water (DI) were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific India Pvt. Ltd. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), 2-hydroxypropane-1,2,3-tricarboxylic acid and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich Pvt Ltd, USA. Polyester woven mesh supplied by a native cloth supplier which was used as a membrane back support.

Briefly, 2 g powdered 2-hydroxypropane-1,2,3-tricarboxylic acid was measured and put in a beaker and heated at 195 °C. After 12 min, the colorless powder form changed into a to pale yellow liquid phase were collected and further used. Next, 10 mg mL−1 solution of NaOH (100 mL) was prepared in another beaker. For obtaining an aqueous GQDs solution, the yellow liquid solution of 2-hydroxypropane-1,2,3-tricarboxylic acid was added dropwise with continuous stirring into the NaOH solution, up until the pH (pH ∼ 7) was neutralized (Dong et al., 2012).

The GQDs/MnO2 nanocomposite was prepared by incorporating certain quantity of synthesized GQDs solution during the process of MnO2 nanoparticles synthesis. An aqueous solution was obtained by mixing MnCl2 in DI water (solution 1). Next, in another beaker certain amount of KMnO4 was added in DI water followed by few drops of concentrated HNO3 (solution 2). Now, the prepared GQDs solution was added to solution 1 and kept for 25 min in an ultrasonic chamber. After that solution 2 was drop by drop mixed in solution 1 and refluxed for 15 h at 100 °C. The resultant blackish-brown precipitate was separated and washed several times with DI water until the pH neutralized (pH ∼ 7). The resultant GQDs/MnO2 was dried at 120 °C followed by calcination at 300 °C (Gupta et al., 2021).

CA was desiccated at 70 °C in a laboratory oven for 50 min prior use. To prepare the TFC-MnO2, TFC-GQD/MnO2-1, TFC-GQD/MnO2-2 and TFC-GQD/MnO2-3 substrates, certain measures of GQDs/MnO2 were first dissolved in NMP with basic stirring followed by sonication for an hour. After complete mixing certain amount of CA and PVP were added to the prepared solution, the casting solution was mixed continuously on a magnetic stirrer for 21 h to acquire homogeneous viscous liquid. At that point, the acquired suspension was left at room temperature to eliminate the presence of air bubbles for 2 h. The detailed casting compositions of the TFC FO membranes are shown in Table 1 . To form TFC FO membranes, the obtained suspension was spread on a fine glass plate utilizing 100 μm fabricating blade and immersed DI water tank at room temperature for PI. Afterwards, to totally eliminate the remaining solvent, the membrane was stored in a new DI water tank for the filtration experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nanoparticles and nanocomposite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) studies of nanocomposites and TFC FO membranes was done utilizing a PerkinElmer spectrometer in attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode. FT-IR spectrums of the prepared samples were obtained in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The membranes were recorded in sets of three, specimen spectrum has been shown for every membrane as no substantial variations were experienced.

The X-ray power diffraction (XRD) patterns of samples were obtained by a pan analytical XPERT-PRO diffractometer, operated at 45 kV and 40 mA with Cu K-α radiation. The samples were recorded in the range of 2θ = 5–75° with a step size of 0.01°.

To examine the surface morphology and the porous structures present in the TFC FO membranes, scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6510LV, JEOL) was used. Further, for confirming the presence of nanomaterials at the membrane surface, electron-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping mode was employed (AMETEK materials analysis division).

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was utilized to explore the surface topography characteristics like surface roughness, mean roughness and peak heights of the TFC FO membranes. To do the investigations, an AFM module (NT -MDT -INTEGRA) with the scanning probe microscope was utilized. The study was done at room temperature in contact mode utilizing a delicate cantilever. The images were recorded in duplicates, and there was no significant difference.

For studying the thermal stability of the TFC FO membrane, their thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, DTA, SII 6300 EXSTAR) was carried out. Membranes were progressively warmed up from ambient to 600 °C at a guarded warming pace of 10 °C/min in a nitrogen (N2) atmosphere. The membranes lasting weight variations with the change in temperature was observed for the thermogravimetric analysis.

Water contact angles (WCA) estimations was done to find out the surface hydrophilicity of membranes using EASYDROP (Kruss) with ±0.1° accuracy. The porosity (Φ %) of membranes was estimated to analyze the ease of filtration by gravimetric analysis. The wet membranes were weighed after gently straining out the excess water present on the membrane surface by using filter paper. The wet membranes were then dried at 45 °C in a laboratory oven and re-weighted. The thickness of the membranes was measured with the help of digital micrometer and the dry-wet weights were measured on an analytical balance twice for the samples. The membrane porosity was measured as per Eq. (1) (Jang et al., 2020):
(1)
Φ % =
W w
−
W d
ρ × A × t
where, Ww and Wd are the weights (g) of wet and dry membrane, respectively, ρ is the density of water (1.00 g cm3), A is the effective membrane area (cm2) and t is the thickness (μm) of the membrane.

The tortuosity (ƭ) of the TFC FO membranes was estimated as per Eq. (2) (Song et al., 2011):
(2)
where, t is the membrane thickness (μm), ɸ is the membrane porosity and S is the membrane structural parameter.

Laboratory scale flat sheet FO treatment unit (Tech INC.) was utilized to perform filtration tests as depicted in Fig. 1 . The FO framework comprises of a rectangular dense stainless-steel FO membrane cell, flow meters, two independent pumps, two tanks (feed side (FS) and draw side (DS)) and a weighing balance for data logging. The FO test cell consists of two equivalent compartments split by FO membrane with effective membrane area of 121.3 cm2. In this study, the experiments were conducted in FO, i.e., active layer facing feed side (AL-FS) mode.

The change in DS volume over the long haul was utilized to calculate the water flux (Jw) in L.m−2.h−1 (LMH) as per Eq. (3) (Xu et al., 2019)
(3)
J w

Am . Δ t
where, ΔV is the change in volume of FS (L) over a specific period, Δt (hr), and Am is the membrane effective area (m2). The osmotic gradient between the FS and DS causes salt diffusion opposite to the flow of water which is measured as reverse salt flux (Js) in g hr m2 (GMH) utilizing conductivity meter (Make: HQ40d, Model: Hach Inc.) as indicated in Eq. (4) (Xu et al., 2019)

where, C0 and V0 are the FS initial concentration (g L−1) and DS initial volume (L), respectively, Ct and Vt are FS salt concentration and measured DS volume (L) at Δt time interval (hr) and Am is the membrane effective area (m2), respectively.

The membrane water permeability coefficient (A) and salt permeability coefficient (B) was estimated on the basis of data obtained from four varying initial draw solution concentrations. The experiment was performed utilizing DI water and NaCl salt solution as FS and DS respectively. The DS concentration was varied at every stage by changing the concentration of NaCl salt solution (0.25M, 0.5M, 1M, and 2M respectively.) Membrane structural parameter (S) was calculated as per Eq. Salt rejection (R) was estimated by the concentration variation from DS and FS, where the concentrations were taken as the average of the initial concentration and the final concentration at the end of the sampling time interval. The salt rejection (R %) was calculated according to Eq. (6) (Jang et al., 2020)

where, C1 is the initial salt concentration, and C0 is the permeate salt concentration computed drew on the amount of water drifted from the feed solution to the draw solution.

The phase structure of the incorporated MnO2 and GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposite was also observed by its XRD spectrum (Fig. 2 a & b). The diffraction patterns of all membranes concur with the XRD peak list of tetragonal MnO2 (JCPDS No.00-044-0141) (Jia et al., 2018). The sharp peaks at 2 θ around 21° and 28° relates to the (002) (De Gruyter, 2020) impression of GQDs. XRD patterns affirm GQDs-MnO2 with blended amorphous and crystalline parts.

Three typical peaks at 2 θ around 27.3°, 36.1°, and 58.6° can be recorded to MnO2. The peaks at 2θ around 36.1° relating to the (211) (Jeong et al., 2019) plane of MnO2 can't be seen after GQDs were incorporated, demonstrating that the stacking of the graphene dots was disorderly. Contrasted with MnO2, the diffraction spectra of MnO2/GQDs had been changed. It is not, at this point unadulterated MnO2 stages; the shapes of the peaks are generously equivalent. At the comparing diffraction points, there are peaks of both GQD and MnO2. In this manner, it tends to be reasoned that the presence of GQDs can't change the crystalline structure of MnO2. The above investigation shows that the nanocomposites are composite with two-phase structure with MnO2 as the prime phase. Similarly, the XRD diffractogram of TFC FO membranes demonstrated very much characterized wide peaks at 2θ around 16–22° which can be linked to cellulose acetate crystalline nature (Jain et al., 2021a). In addition, it shows a formless mound at around 20° with no sharp peaks. This wide halo comes from cellulose acetate group, while absence of sharp GQDs-MnO2 peaks may be because of low weight fraction of nanocomposites, which was utilized during the fabrication of TFC membranes. It clearly shows that the diffused peaks presumably because of the addition of CA, diminishing the crystallinity through the hydrogen bonding among acetyl and hydroxyl groups. The diffraction patterns of nanocomposites in the TFC membrane are not highlighted as the amorphous CA is been dominated.

FT-IR technique was utilized for evaluating the functional groups and its interactions. As indicated in Fig. 3 a, it can be seen that the typical FT-IR spectra of MnO2 nanoparticles shows up in the region of 540–600 cm−1 and the stretch is brought about by the symmetric extending vibration of Mn–O bond (Wang et al., 2007).

The bend at 1400 cm−1 was ascribed to C–H and the bend at 1580 cm−1 was relegated to CC extending vibration of benzene ring, that exhibited the orchestrated GQDs contained ring (Kalluri et al., 2018; Mathur et al., 2021). The typical stretch at 3345 cm−1 was credited to the extending bonding of hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxylate (R–COO-) bonds at 1650 cm−1. The range of prepared membranes within the wavenumber change from 3999 to 398 cm−1 as represented in Fig. 3(b). It shows the representative peaks of CA, PVP and GQDs/MnO2 nanocomposite which indicate ⁓3440 (–OH stretch), 1730 (–CO stretch), 2916 (–CH stretch), 1360 (–CH–bending), 1218 (–C–O– stretch), 1038 (–C–O– stretch), 896 (=C–H bending) (Colburn et al., 2018) glycoside connection among the glucose molecules that shapes the CA polymeric chain and 596 cm−1 may be assigned to the qualities of cellulose acetate derivative groups. It also presents the corresponding bond of CO and O–CO extending at 1700 cm−1, the vibration band of aromatic benzene (CC) at1600 cm−1 and the wide peak of O-H vibrations from hydroxyl and carboxyl group around 2500 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1 (Arthisree and Joshi, 2017). The peak at exactly 538 cm−1 corresponds to the Mn-O bond (Kolta et al., 1971), which confirms the presence of MnO2 structure among the membrane. The retention bands s at 1344, 1174, 1160 and 1042 cm−1 shows the O-H group (Mylarappa et al., 2016) vibrations got together with Mn particles, which recommends the presence of absorbed water molecules with the MnO2 structure. IR spectrums of the composite membranes showed subjectively comparative IR peaks spectra yet little variation (±4 cm−1) in the recurrence locales. This perception is because of the synthetic association of hydrogen bolding among the CA and GQD/MnO2 functional groups which creates a shift in the spectra. The FT-IR examination here is reliable with the outcomes revealed in the literature. Furthermore, the band situated around 1644 cm−1 can be attributed to the extending vibration of the CO (Borodko et al., 2006) in the pyrrolidone group. Likewise, bands at 1279 cm−1 (Safo et al., 2019) which are identified with C–N twisting vibration from the pyrrolidone structure could also be distinguished.

Fig. 4 (a–d) shows the SEM-EDS and elemental mapping images of the prepared TFC FO membranes. As, it can be seen from the images, all the membranes showed characteristic asymmetrical structural morphology. The top surfaces of the membranes reveal dense surface morphology with a few small pores.

Further, on increasing the GQDs-MnO2 blend ratio in the membrane showed noticeable variation in the morphology of the substrates. In addition, it was obvious to see that the GQDs-MnO2 turned out to be a lot more grounded with the expansion in mass proportion, which implied that the concentration of nanocomposites was increased in the TFC membranes. As, it can be found in figure, the top skin-layer in GQDs-MnO2-2 and GQDs-MnO2-3 are somewhat thicker than the other two membranes. This could be credited to more homogenous distribution and solid interaction of GQDs-MnO2 with CA network, which decreases the exchange rate of solvent/non-solvent, when low GQDs-MnO2/MnO2 nanoparticles were joined (Tasselli, 2015). At high nanocomposites stacking, the hydrophilicity of the casting blend increase, and subsequently, the PI exchange rate additionally increases, which brings about noticeable difference in the surface morphology (Purkait et al., 2018). This is likely because of the development of extensive hydrogen bonding among CA, PVP and nanocomposites, PVP encourages the uniform distribution of nanoparticles inside the casting blend, thus, enhancing the nanoparticles interaction with the CA lattice. The EDS investigation portrays the distribution and confinement Mn, C and O elements, which are the fundamental segments of all the TFC FO membranes. The membrane components can be distinguished by purple/blue, red and green spot lights, represents Mn, C and O elements, respectively. From Figure, it tends to be seen that nanoparticles scatter well in the membrane matrix and no significant cluster can be distinguished, which in turn concludes the absence of surface agglomeration of particles. The EDS spectrum of the TFC FO membranes more intense elements peaks with increasing MnO2/GQDs-MnO2 concentrations on its surface.

The AFM images of the TFC FO membranes are depicted in Fig. 5 (a–d). The average surface roughness, mean roughness, maximum peak height, maximum pit depth and maximum height of the membrane surface were analyzed using AFM and presented in Table 2 .

The surface roughness of the TFC FO membranes is known as one of the significant variables influencing the fouling resistance. Membranes with a smooth and uniform surface tends to decreased fouling contrasted with one with high surface harshness. Foulants will in general get absorb and accumulate on the membrane surface, causing a transition decrease in water flux (Kumar and Ismail, 2015). The surface roughness of the GQDs-MnO2 membranes diminishes steadily with the expanded weight fractions of GQDs-MnO2 nanocomposites. As can be noticed, the roughness of the TFC surface improves with the GQDs-MnO2 expansion. The surface roughness is enhanced because of the high hydrophilicity in the GQDs-MnO2 membrane that builds the solvent/non-solvent rate of exchange that brings about higher surface roughness of the created FO membranes. In any case, on account of TFC GQDs-MnO2-2 and TFC GQDs-MnO2-3 membranes, diminished surface roughness is noticed, that can be identified with the nanocomposites amassing in the membrane structure, such that during phase inversion, they can't get on the top of membrane surface (Johnson and Hilal, 2015). On account of hydrophilic membrane structure, the wettability of TFC surface enhances with expanding surface roughness. The hypothesis is steady with the AFM analysis, pure water flux and contact angles and as can be observed, the roughness of TFC -MnO2 is more than that of TFC GQDs-MnO2-1, and the other way around, the contact angles of TFC -MnO2 is not as much as TFC GQDs-MnO2-1 and affirms the connection among wettability and roughness of the TFC surface. This investigation uncovers that the presence of GQDs-MnO2 composite bringing about a smooth surface of the TFC FO membranes. Also, the smooth surface of the GQDs-MnO2 membranes will have a superior antifouling ability. It is clearly indicated that the surface roughness of the TFC is diminished with the increment of nanocomposite loading in the FO membranes.

TGA and DTGA show that all the samples exhibited three distinct weight loss stages at 50 ⁰C-650 °C (Fig. 6 (a–d)). However, major loss in weights is observed (∼50 wt%) in the range of 350 °C - 400 °C for all the samples, which is ascribed to the structural dissipation of TFC-FO membranes (because of the loss of hydroxyl groups and depolymerization of CA to anhydroglucose units).

First order derivative of TGA curves reveals the degree at which the maximum mass decomposition occurs. The maximum degree of weight loss is around 484 °C for the TFC-MnO2 membrane, 486 °C for the TFC-GQD/MnO2-1 membrane, 518 °C for the TFC-GQD/MnO2-2 membrane, 497 °C for the TFC-GQD/MnO2-3 membrane. DTGA data clearly show that endothermic peaks are due to thermal degradation because of the continuous loss in CA weight fraction and chain-scission reactions, which in turn requires high temperature. From the TG/DTA data it is observed that GQD/MnO2-3 membrane is highly stable. The stability of membranes increases with increasing % of nanocomposite.

Table 3 sums up the structural parameters of the fabricated TFC FO membranes, which are utilized to analyze the intrinsic separation performance of the membranes despite the external operating conditions. For an enhanced assessment, the corresponding S-values were determined at four variable initial DS concentrations, namely 0.25M NaCl, 0.5M NaCl, 1M NaCl, and 2M NaCl. As detailed in the literature, the FO membrane structural parameters include water permeability (A), salt permeability (B), thickness (t), tortuosity (τ), and porosity (ɸ) of the membrane substrate. Therefore, the prepared membranes were also tested in RO test cell with an applied pressure of 2.5 bar with DI water used as FS. From Table 3, the minimal τ value and the maximum ɸ value is obtained for the TFC-GQD/MnO2-2 which results in the minimal S value for the TFC-GQD/MnO2-2 membrane. Generally, the minimal S value is considered acceptable as it decrease the ICP and at the same time increases water permeability in the FO process (Kim et al., 2017). In the view of obtained results, the incorporation of the GQD/MnO2 nanocomposite leads to formation of the membranes with the smaller S compared to the MnO2 and 0.3% GQD/MnO2 membrane S value. The above outcome shows that the GQD/MnO2 nanocomposite TFC membranes possess greater water permeability and salt rejection in comparison to the MnO2 and 0.3% GQD/MnO2 TFC membrane, and are anticipated for superior FO performance.

It is clear from Table 3 that the A value of the TFC FO MnO2 membrane is equal to 1.90 LMH. bar− 1 on applying a pressure of 2.5 bar. On adding the GQDs nanocomposite from 0.3 wt% up to 0.7%, the A value of the membranes increases from 1.94 to 2.05 LMH. Bar−1 (for the TFC FO GQD/MnO2), gradually. It is also noted that, further enhancing the nanocomposite content to 0.7%, decreases A value from 2.05 to 2.00 LMH bar− 1 due to high nanoparticles concentration which might have resulted in pore blocking (Agi et al., 2018). Similarly, the membranes modified with GQDs nanocomposite shows higher NaCl rejection and lower B value in comparison to the MnO2 membrane. It can be observed that adding GQDs/MnO2 up to 0.5 wt% significantly enhance the structural characteristics of membrane like water permeability and hydrophilicity. Moreover, the addition of GQDs enhances the surface roughness of the membrane which is considered as an important factor for estimation of salt rejection, which concludes that at 0.5 wt% of GQD/MnO2, the TFC FO membrane has comparable good characteristics with highest A value and lowest B value. This perhaps can be associated with the following factors like, adding GQDs along with MnO2 in the membranes which increased the membranes surface hydrophilicity, that attracted more water molecules to pass through the membrane matrix and facilitated their transport (Mat Nawi et al., 2020). Next, the incorporation of nanocomposite contributes to a minimal transport resistance and enhanced osmotic flux (Wen et al., 2019). Moreover, it might have created additional nano-channels for water molecules transport through interfacial gap among GQD/MnO2 and CA (Mahmoud et al., 2015). What's more, MnO2 is a stratified with many water molecules, hence, joining the GQDs with MnO2 may provide extensive hydrogen bonds among the carboxyl, hydroxyl and oxygen-containing functional groups of the GQDs/MnO2 (Zhang et al., 2020). Similarly, MnO2 can establish bonds with oxygenated functional groups, hence, the nanocomposite effectively inhibits the agglomeration of MnO2 and at the same time facilitates higher water permeability during the FO process. The higher water permeability and hydrophilicity of GQDs/MnO2 TFC FO membranes can also be established by the WCA result from Fig. 7 . As can be seen, the WCA of the TFC FO membranes decreases steadily with the addition of GQD/MnO2 loading in the membrane structure, suggesting that the nanocomposite membrane with oxygen-containing functional groups improves the membrane hydrophilicity notably and results in greater osmotic water flux.

The durability and structural integrity of the TFC FO membranes was examined by soaking in 0.2 M NaCl salt solution utilizing free-standing membrane. It is observed that the TFC FO MnO2 membrane was little impaired and started crumbling from day 62 and the TFC-GQD/MnO2-1 membrane was impaired from day 85 whereas the TFC-GQD/MnO2-2 and TFC-GQD/MnO2-3 membranes were intact and sustained their initial structure for 96 days without any impairment and damage. The results further indicate enhanced durability and structural integrity on increasing the nanocomposite concentration (Fig. 8 ).

Fig. 9 represent the separation performance of TFC FO membranes with different nanocomposite loadings. The higher osmotic water flux (Jw) and reverse salt flux (Js) of all fabricated membranes in FO mode demonstrate different degree of dilutive and concentrative ICPs as a result of net osmotic pressure difference (Fig. 9) (Gray et al., 2006). The control TFC FO MnO2 membrane showed 34.23 LMH water flux, lower than that of TFC-GQD/MnO2-1 (37.31 LMH), TFC-GQD/MnO2-2 (42.43 LMH), and TFC-GQD/MnO2-3 (40.32 LMH) membranes (Fig. 9(a)). However, the decreasing water flux of TFC-GQD/MnO2-3 membrane is achieved when the nanocomposite concentration increases, which may be because of the reduction in pure water permeability of the membrane at increased GQD/MnO2 concentration (Table 3). These variations in the osmotic water flux may also be associated with the increase in WCA (Fig. 7) and the combined effect of the enhanced hydrophilicity, thinner selective layer and additional passages formed as the concentration of GQD/MnO2 increases.

The reverse salt flux (Js) is also a key parameter to assess the performance of FO membranes. From a commercial viewpoint also, it is a repulsive parameter which functions as genesis for fouling and scaling of the membranes. As shown in Fig. 9(b), the Js of the control TFC FO MnO2 membrane is observed as 5.34 gMH. And as we go ahead, the Js values of the TFC-GQD/MnO2-1 (5.98 gMH), TFC-GQD/MnO2-2 (6.02 gMH) and TFC-GQD/MnO2-3 (6.12 gMH) membranes increases as nanocomposite concentration increases, that restrict the movement of salt molecules. The above observations may also be backed by the enhanced salt rejection (R%) and decreased B/A ratio at more GQD/MnO2 loading (Table 3).

On the basis of the Jw and Js results, it is still difficult to predict the best performance of FO membrane because of trade-off relationship among osmotic flux and reverse salt flux. Therefore, the reverse solute flux selectivity (RSFS) (Jw/Js) for all the fabricated membranes was measured as it relies on the osmotic pressure and selectivity of the FO membranes (Phillip et al., 2010). Fig. 9(c) depicts the RSFS for fabricated membranes which shows that out of all the membranes, TFC-GQD/MnO2-2 is showing the highest value of RSFS 4.65 L g−1 in FO mode.

Meanwhile, the result of the DS concentration on the water flux and salt rejection of TFC FO membranes was also recorded. From Fig. 10 , it can be observed that both water flux and salt rejection of the prepared membranes reveal analogous trends with the increase in the DS concentration from 0.25 M to 2 M NaCl salt solution. The water flux is found to gradually increase as the DS concentration increases, which is allocated to higher osmotic force driven by the high DS concentration. The higher water flux along with decreased water penetration resistance is observed as a result of enhanced surface hydrophilicity as well as morphology of the membranes due to the presence of rich functional groups in GQD/MnO2 nanocomposite. Further, it has been reported that higher surface roughness is also considered as an implied parameter on water flux (Azizian and Khosravi, 2019).

For stronger observation, the FO performance of the best membrane (TFC-GQD/MnO2-2) fabricated in this study along with some graphene-based membranes detailed in the literature is presented in Table 4 . It can be seen that the TFC-GQD/MnO2-2 membrane having 0.5 wt% GQDs/MnO2 nanocomposite with comparable Js and Jw value reveal excellent FO performance, which is either superior than or as-good-as with some of the other membranes. Hence, the GQDs/MnO2 nanocomposite can be proposed to be utilized for superior FO membrane fabrication for desalination and water purification applications.

In this work, novel MnO2 incited GQDs incorporated TFC FO membranes were prepared by phase inversion process. The prepared membranes were characterized using FT-IR, XRD, SEM-EDS, AFM, and TGA techniques and results indicated the formation of functional and highly water permeable membranes. Compared with the unmodified TFC FO MnO2 membrane, introducing GQDs in the membrane can affect the structural formation of membranes. The GQD/MnO2 nanocomposite membrane with oxygen-containing functional groups showed improved membrane hydrophilicity, durability and structural integrity of membranes on increasing the nanocomposite concentration. Also, the smooth surface of the GQDs-MnO2 membranes will have a superior antifouling ability as the surface roughness of the membranes is diminished with the increment in the nanocomposite loading, which improves the FO separation performance of the nanocomposite membranes. Consequently, the TFC FO GQD/MnO2 membranes exhibited improved water permeability (2.05 LMH bar−1), enhanced surface morphology and superior water flux (42.34 LMH) and salt rejection (96.87%). These results show that GQDs/MnO2 nanocomposite is a promising material for high-performance FO membranes. This work provides a novel insight into the fabrication of membranes for improved FO separation performance.
